5 Production Failures I’ve Watched Happen at 5 Different Companies. The Root Cause Was the Same Every Time.

The shapes don’t change. Only the stack does.

10 min read
Just now

--

The first time I saw a retry storm take down a production system, I was at a fintech startup with twelve engineers and a payment processing service that had been running reliably for two years.
The second time I saw a retry storm take down a production system, I was at an enterprise software company with four hundred engineers and a microservices architecture that had taken three years to build.
The third time, I was consulting for a healthcare platform whose engineers had read every distributed systems book I would have recommended and had deliberately designed retry logic with exponential backoff.
Different companies. Different stacks. Different teams. Different levels of engineering sophistication.
Same failure. Same mechanism. Same outcome.
That consistency is the thing I keep coming back to.
Production failures are not random. They have shapes. The shapes repeat across organizations that share nothing except the fundamental patterns of how distributed systems fail under real conditions.
Once you recognize the shape, the specific technology becomes almost irrelevant. The failure is the same. The diagnostic path is the same. The fix addresses the same root cause.
These are five failures I have watched happen more than once. Each one at a different company. Each one with the same underlying shape.
Failure One: The Retry Storm
Company one: A fintech startup. Payment processing service. Tuesday afternoon traffic spike. One downstream fraud detection API starts returning slow responses. Upstream payment service retries failed requests immediately, three times, with no delay. Fraud detection API receives three times its normal load from retries alone. Fraud detection API slows further. Payment service retries more aggressively. Fraud detection API falls over completely. Payment service has no circuit breaker. Every payment attempt retries against a down service until timeout. Checkout is down. Revenue stops. Three hours to recovery because nobody knows what’s causing the load spike on the fraud detection service.
Company two: Enterprise software. Microservices architecture. Notification service degrades during a scheduled maintenance window on a dependent messaging platform. Seventeen upstream services all configured with similar retry logic begin retrying simultaneously. Messaging platform receives seventeen times its normal load at the exact moment it is trying to recover from maintenance. Maintenance window extends by four hours. Every upstream service’s error rate climbs as retries fail. What should have been a thirty-minute maintenance window becomes a four-hour incident.
Company three: Healthcare platform. Explicit exponential backoff implemented. Retry delays of one second, two seconds, four seconds. The team was proud of this implementation. During a downstream service degradation, the exponential backoff prevented the immediate amplification. The retry storm arrived thirty seconds later when every upstream service’s one-second retry fired simultaneously, then every two-second retry, then every four-second retry. Synchronized backoff without jitter produces synchronized retry waves instead of distributed load.
The shape across all three: a downstream service degradation amplified by upstream retry logic into a total failure. The mechanism changes slightly. The shape does not.
The fix that works: exponential backoff with jitter, plus circuit breakers that stop retrying against a service that is clearly down, plus retry budgets that limit the total number of retries any service can generate in a given time window.
The diagnostic question that finds it in two minutes: is traffic to the failing service increasing or decreasing as the failure gets worse? If it’s increasing, your own systems are amplifying the failure.

Failure Two: The Silent Data Corruption
Company one: E-commerce platform. Order management service. @Transactional annotation on a private method called internally within the same Spring service. The proxy that Spring uses to implement transaction management is bypassed by internal calls. The annotation does nothing. Orders that fail during processing leave inventory reserved but the order in an inconsistent state. The corruption is small enough to pass application validation and large enough to cause inventory discrepancies. Runs for eight months before a quarterly inventory audit finds it. Fix is six lines. Cleanup takes six weeks.
Company two: Financial services. Account balance service. Two microservices writing to the same account record under concurrent load. No distributed lock. No optimistic locking. Read-modify-write pattern executed by both services simultaneously. Each service reads the current balance, calculates an update, writes the result. Under concurrent load, the second write overwrites the first. Account balances are occasionally wrong by the amount of one transaction. Runs for fourteen months before a compliance audit finds it.
Company three: SaaS platform. User subscription service. Database migration script that runs as part of deployment. Migration acquires a table lock, performs updates, releases the lock. During the migration, writes from the application are blocked but the application doesn’t know they’re blocked. It retries. The lock releases. The migration updates complete. The application retries complete. Some rows are updated twice. The duplication is in a non-idempotent field. Data is wrong. Nobody knows until a customer calls support about billing.
The shape across all three: a write operation that is not atomic when it needs to be, producing data that fails quietly rather than loudly. The error is in the data, not in the application. The application believes it succeeded because no exception was thrown. The data says otherwise.
The fix that works: explicit transaction boundaries that are verified rather than assumed, distributed locking or optimistic locking for concurrent writes, idempotency in write operations that might be retried.
The diagnostic question that finds it before it happens: what does our data state look like if this write operation succeeds halfway? Walk through the code path and identify every state the data can be in at each step. If any intermediate state is inconsistent, there is a corruption path.

Failure Three: The Connection Pool Exhaustion
Company one: Media streaming platform. Video metadata service. Default HikariCP configuration deployed to production. Maximum pool size of ten connections. Normal load uses six connections comfortably. During a content release that drove ten times normal traffic, all ten connections are in use. New requests queue for connections. Queue timeout is thirty seconds. Requests that wait thirty seconds return errors. Error rate climbs. More users retry. More connection requests queue. The database team says the database is healthy. It is. The pool is exhausted. The investigation goes to the database for ninety minutes before someone checks the pool metrics.
Company two: B2B SaaS. Reporting service. A new feature introduces a query that holds a database connection for forty-five seconds while it aggregates a large dataset. Under normal load, only a few users run reports simultaneously and the pool handles it. A marketing email drives a surge of users who all run reports at the same time. Every report holds a connection for forty-five seconds. The pool exhausts in thirty seconds. All report requests queue. All report requests timeout. No errors in the database. No errors in the query. The connection was held too long.
Company three: Fintech. Transaction history service. A connection leak introduced by a code change that opens a connection in a try block and closes it in a finally block, but the finally block has a code path that can throw an exception before the connection is closed. Under normal operation, the exception path is rare. During an unusual sequence of events in production, the exception fires consistently. Connections leak one at a time. The pool drains over ninety minutes. The service fails. The leak detection threshold is not configured. The leak is invisible until the pool is empty.
The shape across all three: the application cannot get a database connection because the pool is exhausted. The database is healthy. The errors mention the database. The investigation goes to the database. The answer is in the pool.
The diagnostic metric that finds it in ninety seconds: pool utilization trend over the thirty minutes before the incident. If the pool was trending toward its maximum before the alert fired, the cause is resource exhaustion, not database failure.
The configuration that prevents it: connection pool size tuned to production load, leak detection threshold configured, pool metrics on the primary monitoring dashboard.

Failure Four: The Deployment That Only Fails in Production
Company one: Consumer app. Notification service. Environment variable for the push notification provider API key set in development, staging, and QA environments. Not set in production. The deployment succeeds. The service starts. The first push notification attempt fails with an authentication error. Every push notification attempt fails. The error message mentions the push notification provider. The investigation goes to the push notification provider. Their status page shows no incidents. Their API is healthy. The environment variable is missing. The investigation finds it after two hours.
Company two: Enterprise platform. Reporting service. Database connection string uses a specific character encoding in development and staging. Production database uses a different encoding. The difference is invisible in most queries. It appears when users submit content with specific Unicode characters. The content is truncated silently. No error is thrown. The data is wrong. Users in specific locales notice. Support tickets accumulate over weeks before the encoding difference is found.
Company three: Healthcare platform. Integration service. Third-party EHR system has different rate limits in its sandbox environment versus production. The integration is tested thoroughly in the sandbox. In production, the rate limit is one-tenth of the sandbox rate limit. Under normal load, the rate limit is occasionally hit and retried successfully. During a high-volume period, the rate limit is hit consistently. Retries hit the rate limit again immediately. The service enters a retry loop against a rate limit wall. The integration stops processing. The difference between sandbox and production rate limits was not documented by the vendor.
The shape across all three: the code is correct, the environment is not what the code expects. The failure only appears in production because only production has the specific environmental difference that triggers it.
The diagnostic question that finds it: what is different about the production environment that does not exist in any other environment? List every environment-specific configuration value and verify each one is correctly set in production. This question is uncomfortable because the list is often longer than anyone realizes.
The prevention that works: environment parity as a practice, not a goal. Every exception to environment parity is documented as a deliberate decision with a known risk.

Failure Five: The Cascade That Looks Like a Traffic Spike
Company one: E-commerce platform. Product catalog service degrades during a peak traffic period. Response times increase from fifty milliseconds to three seconds. Upstream services have timeouts set to five seconds. Requests to the catalog service block threads for up to five seconds waiting for a response. Thread pools in upstream services fill with blocked threads. Upstream services
